planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained woodland soil in deep shade under deciduous trees. Cutleaf Toothwort goes completely dormant by late spring — mark its location to avoid disturbing the dormant rhizomes during summer garden work.

directions: Cutleaf Toothwort is a native spring ephemeral of moist, rich, deciduous woodlands across eastern North America, producing clusters of small white to pale pink flowers on 6–15 inch stems in early to mid-spring, shortly before the forest canopy closes and blocks the light that spring ephemerals depend on. The deeply cut, three-parted leaves with their toothlike lobes give the plant its common name and are an identification feature distinguishing it from the similar Slender Toothwort; the leaves appear in early spring, the plant flowers, sets seed, and retreats entirely underground by early summer, remaining dormant until the following winter. A member of the mustard family, the rhizomes have a mild, peppery, horseradish-like flavor and were eaten raw by various Native American peoples. Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in deep shade; Cutleaf Toothwort self-seeds freely in hospitable woodland gardens and gradually colonizes the forest floor.

planting.note: Plant clump divisions in early spring in moist to moderately dry, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in deep to partial shade; Mottled Wild Ginger is slow-growing and long-lived. Maintain consistent moisture until established, then reduce watering as the plant is drought-tolerant in deep shade once its root system is developed.

directions: Mottled Wild Ginger is a beautiful native evergreen ground cover of the southern Appalachians and upper coastal plain, producing low mounds of heart-shaped, leathery leaves decorated with silver and pale green mottling against dark green — one of the most ornamentally spectacular native shade plants of the southeastern United States. Unlike the more northern Asarum canadense, which is deciduous, Mottled Wild Ginger retains its attractively patterned foliage through winter and forms a weed-suppressing carpet in dry to moist shade where few other plants grow reliably year-round. The curious, urn-shaped brownish-purple flowers are produced near the soil surface in late spring, hidden beneath the foliage where they are pollinated primarily by ground beetles and ants attracted to their earthy fragrance. Plant divisions in spring in moist to moderately dry, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in deep to partial shade; plants spread very slowly but are virtually permanent once established, gradually forming dense colonies that suppress weeds under deciduous trees.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant fibrous root divisions in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly to moderately calcareous, humus-rich, stony woodland soil in deep shade. Sharp-Lobed Hepatica prefers richer, more base-rich soils than Round-Lobed Hepatica. Do not plant deeply — the crown should be at or just below the soil surface.

directions: Sharp-Lobed Hepatica is one of the first native wildflowers to bloom in the eastern woodlands, producing delicate white, pink, blue, or lavender flowers on slender, hairy stems in late winter to early spring — often while frost still covers the ground — before the deciduous forest canopy has begun to leaf out. The three-lobed, leathery leaves remain green through the winter as an evergreen basal rosette, often acquiring beautiful reddish-purple tones in the cold months; distinguished from Round-Lobed Hepatica by the pointed (acute) tips of its three leaf lobes, it prefers slightly richer, more alkaline woodland soils with good calcium content. A long-lived, slow-growing wildflower of exceptional garden character, Sharp-Lobed Hepatica is best established from fall-planted divisions of existing clumps rather than from seed, which requires 2–3 years of stratification cycles before germination. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly calcareous, humus-rich soil with some grit or stone in deep shade under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant fibrous root divisions in fall in moist to moderately dry, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich, stony woodland soil in deep shade. Round-Lobed Hepatica tolerates somewhat drier, sandier, and more acidic soils than Sharp-Lobed Hepatica and is often found in drier oak-hickory woods.

directions: Round-Lobed Hepatica is a beloved native wildflower of eastern North American forests, producing delicate flowers ranging from white through pink to lavender and blue in late winter to very early spring, often blooming while snow still lingers around its base and becoming one of the first wildflowers to greet emerging bees and butterflies each year. The three-lobed, mottled green and reddish leaves remain evergreen through winter as a low basal rosette, distinguished from the closely related Sharp-Lobed Hepatica by the rounded (obtuse) tips of its three leaf lobes and its preference for drier, more acidic, sandy or stony soils in drier oak-hickory forest rather than the rich, base-rich soils of beech-maple forest. A long-lived perennial of extraordinary persistence — individual plants may live for decades — Round-Lobed Hepatica is best established from fall-planted divisions and takes 2–3 years to settle into its permanent location before flowering reliably each spring. Plant in moist to dry, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich woodland soil in deep shade under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Sarvis Holly is dioecious — both male and female plants are required within 50 feet for berry production. It thrives in low, wet, shaded settings where many other holly species struggle.

directions: Sarvis Holly is a rarely cultivated native deciduous holly of moist, low-lying forests, wet woods, and stream margins of the coastal plain from South Carolina to Louisiana, producing clusters of small pink to red berries that ripen in late summer and fall and are avidly consumed by mockingbirds, bluebirds, robins, and other fruit-eating birds during the critical pre-migration feeding period. The name 'sarvis' is a folk rendering of 'serviceberry' or 'service tree,' reflecting the ripening time of its berries relative to other fall food sources. Less commonly cultivated than Possumhaw or Winterberry hollies, Sarvis Holly thrives in the wet, shaded conditions of bottomland forest and stream banks where larger hollies may struggle, and deserves wider use in native plant landscapes. Plant rooted cuttings in moist to wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; dioecious, so both sexes are required within pollinator range for berry set.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States (Weakley)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. Georgia Holly is dioecious and requires both male and female plants nearby for berry production. It is rare in cultivation but available from specialist native plant nurseries.

directions: Georgia Holly is a rare and regionally endemic native deciduous holly of the upper coastal plain and piedmont, found in a narrow range across Georgia, Alabama, and adjacent states, where it grows as a spreading shrub in the understory of moist, well-drained, sandy-loam upland forest. Distinctive among native hollies for its unusually long flower and fruit pedicels — the scientific epithet longipes means 'long-footed' — the bright red berries appear to float in space on their elongated stalks, making them more visible and accessible to birds than the closely bunched berries of many other holly species. A candidate for greater cultivation in the native plant garden, Georgia Holly is a multi-season ornamental shrub with small white spring flowers, deep green summer foliage, vivid fall berries, and clean branch structure in winter. Plant rooted cuttings in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade; as with all native hollies, both male and female plants must be present for berry production.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States (Weakley)

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to high, shifting shade under deciduous trees. Oconee Azalea prefers somewhat drier, well-drained upland soils and will not tolerate wet feet or alkaline conditions.

directions: Oconee Azalea is a spectacular native deciduous azalea of dry to mesic upland oak-hickory and pine-oak woods of Georgia and South Carolina, producing some of the most vivid orange, red, and scarlet floral displays of any native North American shrub when it blooms in March and April — before or with the emerging leaves — providing a burst of intense color unlike any other native plant in the early spring landscape. The flowers are not fragrant but are intensely colored in shades of flame orange to deep scarlet, and the plant's natural occurrence is restricted to a relatively small area of the inner coastal plain and piedmont of the Carolinas, making it a regionally rare and culturally significant plant of considerable interest to azalea enthusiasts. A moderate-growing shrub that develops a naturally graceful, open, rounded form, Oconee Azalea provides superior wildlife value to most exotic azalea cultivars and supports native bumblebee queens emerging in early spring. Plant in well-drained, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to high, shifting shade; Oconee Azalea will not tolerate wet feet or alkaline soils.
sources: Native Azaleas of the Southeastern United States (Towe)

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in moist, consistently acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to light shade. Pinkshell Azalea requires reliable moisture and good air circulation and does not tolerate dry, alkaline, or compacted soils; it is most reliable in cool, high-humidity environments in Zones 4–7.

directions: Pinkshell Azalea is a beautiful and rare native deciduous azalea endemic to a small area of the Blue Ridge Mountains of North Carolina, where it grows on boggy hillsides, stream banks, and acidic seeps at elevations above 3,000 feet — one of the most restricted native azaleas in the eastern United States, known from fewer than 20 natural populations. The delicate, five-petaled, light to deep pink flowers borne in early spring before leaf emergence have a distinctive butterfly shape lacking the long floral tube typical of most azaleas, making the blooms uniquely recognizable among native azalea enthusiasts and adding exceptional elegance to the spring garden. Among the hardiest of all native azaleas, surviving USDA Zone 4 winters with ease, Pinkshell Azalea is a superb choice for northern gardens seeking a spectacular, native spring-flowering shrub for moist, partially shaded spots. Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade with good air circulation; it requires reliable moisture and high humidity to perform well, especially in warmer zones.
sources: Native Azaleas of the Southeastern United States (Towe)

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to high open shade under deciduous trees. Roseshell Azalea tolerates somewhat drier and rockier conditions than most native azaleas and is the hardiest species, performing well in Zones 3–7.

directions: Roseshell Azalea is a fragrant native deciduous azalea of the northeastern United States and Appalachian Mountains, producing profuse clusters of deep rose-pink to light pink flowers with a strong, sweet clove-like fragrance that perfumes entire gardens in late spring — a quality unique among native azaleas that makes it an outstanding choice wherever fragrance is a garden priority. The most cold-hardy of all native azaleas, surviving USDA Zone 3 reliably, Roseshell Azalea grows in rocky, dry-to-moist, open woods, cliff edges, and forest margins from Quebec south to the Virginia mountains, tolerating somewhat drier and stonier conditions than most other native azalea species. The compact, naturally rounded growth habit, spectacular fragrant flowers, and brilliant orange-red to crimson fall foliage make this a superb multi-season native shrub for northern and mountain landscapes. Plant in well-drained, acidic, humus-rich, rocky or sandy soil in partial shade to high, shifting shade under deciduous trees.
sources: Native Azaleas of the Southeastern United States (Towe)

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in moist to moderately wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. Florida Serviceberry tolerates wet, sandy coastal plain soils where other serviceberries may struggle, and is smaller and more compact than Downy Serviceberry, making it a better fit for smaller gardens.

directions: Florida Serviceberry is a native shrub of moist to wet, sandy coastal plain habitats from Virginia south to Florida, producing clusters of small white flowers in late winter to early spring — among the first native flowers to open each year — that provide a critical early nectar and pollen source for emerging native bees and butterflies at a time when little else is blooming. The sweet, dark blue-purple berries that follow in late spring and early summer have a mild flavor reminiscent of blueberries and are avidly consumed by mockingbirds, catbirds, Baltimore orioles, cedar waxwings, and over 40 other bird species during the critical spring migration feeding period. The smaller, more compact stature compared to the familiar Downy Serviceberry makes Florida Serviceberry well suited to smaller gardens and naturalized plantings in the coastal plain Southeast. Plant in moist to moderately wet, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Florida Serviceberry tolerates the wet, sandy, coastal plain soils that can challenge other native fruit shrubs.
